issue: Amber Blinking Port LED and Link Down
search_summary: SFP+ transceiver fiber optic module port amber blinking LED link down err-disabled GBIC mismatch unsupported.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: An amber blinking port LED on a Cat 9300 typically indicates an err-disabled state, a GBIC mismatch, or an unsupported transceiver. Run `show interfaces status err-disabled` and `show interface transceiver detail` from privileged EXEC. Common causes: non-Cisco-branded SFP rejected by `service unsupported-transceiver` policy, dirty fiber endface, or a failed laser. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Run `show inventory` and confirm the SFP PID is on the Cisco compatibility matrix. Step 2: Clean both fiber endfaces with a 1.25mm one-click cleaner — never reuse alcohol wipes. Step 3: If err-disabled, recover the port: `interface gi1/0/24` then `shutdown` followed by `no shutdown`. Step 4: For unsupported SFPs as a temporary workaround, enter global config: `service unsupported-transceiver` and `no errdisable detect cause gbic-invalid` — note this voids TAC support. Step 5: Confirm Tx/Rx optical power with `show interface gi1/0/24 transceiver detail` — values below -14 dBm Rx indicate a dirty fiber or failed laser. Step 6: If the transceiver still does not link, swap into a known-good port to isolate switch vs. SFP failure.

issue: PSU Fault LED Solid Red and Beeping Alarm
search_summary: Power supply PSU red fault LED beeping audible alarm chassis power redundancy failure hot-swap.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A solid red PSU LED with audible beeping on a Cat 9300 indicates the PSU has failed or is not receiving AC input. The beep alarm fires when only one of two redundant PSUs is operational. Run `show environment power` to confirm the failed slot. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Verify AC input — check the C13 power cord seating and the upstream PDU breaker. Step 2: Inspect the PSU LED: solid red = internal fault, off = no AC, blinking green = standby. Step 3: To hot-swap, ensure the redundant PSU is showing solid green (`OK` in `show environment power`). Step 4: Disconnect the AC cord from the failed unit. Step 5: Loosen the captive thumbscrew and slide the PSU out — wait 10 seconds. Step 6: Slide the replacement PSU in until it clicks, tighten the thumbscrew, and reconnect AC. Step 7: Confirm both PSUs report `OK / Good` in `show environment power`. Step 8: To silence the alarm if you are running with a single PSU intentionally: `power redundancy-mode redundant` becomes `power redundancy-mode combined` in global config.

issue: Three Short Beeps on Boot - No POST
search_summary: Three short beeps boot POST failure beep code memory DIMM detection BIOS Dell PowerEdge motherboard error.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: On the Dell PowerEdge R740, three short beeps at power-on indicate a memory module detection failure (BIOS POST code 1-3-1-1 family). The BMC log via iDRAC will record the failure as `MEM0001` or `MEM0701`. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Power off the server completely and disconnect both PSU power cords — wait 30 seconds for residual charge to drain. Step 2: Open the chassis lid and locate the DIMM banks (12 slots per CPU socket on the R740). Step 3: Cross-reference the lit amber DIMM LED on the motherboard or the iDRAC event log to identify the failed slot (e.g., A1, B5). Step 4: Reseat the suspect DIMM — press straight down with even pressure on both ends until both ejector tabs click closed. Step 5: If the beep persists, swap the suspect DIMM with a known-good one from the same channel — if the fault follows the DIMM, replace it; if it stays at the slot, the slot is faulty. Step 6: Confirm DIMMs are populated per the R740 memory population guide — odd populations cause POST failures. Step 7: Power on and watch the LCD diagnostic panel for the green `System ID` indicator.

issue: Virtual Disk Degraded and Background Initialization
search_summary: PERC RAID controller virtual disk degraded background initialization rebuild failed disk array foreign config.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A degraded virtual disk on the PERC H730P typically means one physical disk in the RAID set is failed, predicted-fail, or marked foreign. Confirm via iDRAC -> Storage -> Virtual Disks or boot into the Ctrl+R PERC BIOS config. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Identify the failed slot: in iDRAC Storage view, look for the disk with state `Failed` or `Predicted Failure`. The corresponding chassis bay LED will be solid amber. Step 2: Confirm spare-slot availability — `Hot Spare` columns will show `Global Spare` if one is configured. Step 3: For hot-swap: simply pull the failed disk out by its release lever; insert the replacement of identical capacity (same or larger) and same interface (SAS/SATA). Step 4: The PERC will auto-start a rebuild if a global hot spare was configured, otherwise the new disk will appear as `Foreign` — clear the foreign config from PERC BIOS or iDRAC: Storage -> Controller -> Foreign Config -> Clear. Step 5: Manually start the rebuild: Storage -> Physical Disks -> select disk -> Assign as Replacement. Step 6: Monitor rebuild progress via iDRAC — typical rebuild time is 4–8 hours per TB. Step 7: Avoid heavy I/O during rebuild as it doubles rebuild time and increases the chance of a second-disk failure.

issue: iLO Web Interface Unreachable and Health LED Amber
search_summary: iLO HPE management controller unreachable web interface amber health LED firmware corrupt reset baseboard BMC.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: An amber HPE health LED with the iLO web interface unreachable on a DL380 Gen10 typically means iLO firmware is hung or corrupt, or an iLO security/IP misconfiguration. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Try a soft iLO reset from the host OS — on Linux: `hponcfg -r` (requires hp-health and hponcfg packages); on Windows: HPE Smart Storage Administrator -> iLO -> Reset. Step 2: If unreachable from OS, perform a hard iLO reset via the chassis: locate the iLO reset pin-hole on the back of the server (next to the iLO RJ45) and depress for 5 seconds with a paperclip. Step 3: Wait 60 seconds for iLO to re-initialise — the heartbeat LED next to the iLO port should change from solid to flashing green. Step 4: If still unreachable, connect a laptop directly to the iLO port and the iLO will fall back to its factory IP (printed on the chassis pull-out tag) — log in with the factory iLO password also on the tag. Step 5: For corrupt firmware, recover via `flash recovery` mode: power off, remove AC, hold the iLO reset button, reapply AC while holding — release after 8 seconds. iLO will boot the recovery image and accept a USB-supplied firmware bin. Step 6: Re-flash the latest iLO firmware from HPE Support. Step 7: Reset the system health log: System Log -> Clear after the underlying issue is resolved.

issue: Routing Engine Mastership Switchover and Loss of Management
search_summary: Juniper EX4300 routing engine RE mastership switchover GRES nonstop routing loss of management failover virtual chassis.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A Routing Engine mastership switchover on an EX4300 (in a Virtual Chassis) causes a brief loss of management plane and may interrupt forwarding if NSR/NSB is not configured. Use `show virtual-chassis status` and `show system commit` to identify which RE became master. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From the master RE, run `show virtual-chassis status` to confirm both REs and member roles (RE0/RE1 or master/backup). Step 2: Check switchover cause: `show system switchover` — common causes include manual `request chassis routing-engine master switch`, kernel panic, or memory exhaustion. Step 3: Verify Graceful Routing Engine Switchover (GRES) is enabled to minimise impact: `set chassis redundancy graceful-switchover` followed by `set routing-options nonstop-routing` and `set system commit synchronize`. Step 4: To force a clean failover back: `request chassis routing-engine master switch check` (validates it is safe), then `request chassis routing-engine master switch`. Step 5: After failover, sync the configuration: `commit synchronize` from the new master. Step 6: Reboot the previously failed RE if it shows hung: `request system reboot other-routing-engine`. Step 7: For management loss on the VC, ensure the VME (Virtual Management Ethernet) interface is configured: `set interfaces vme unit 0 family inet address 10.x.x.x/24`.

issue: ESP Crash and Forwarding Plane Reload
search_summary: Cisco ASR 1001-X ESP embedded services processor crash forwarding plane reload QFP traceback ucode IOSd.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: An ESP crash on the ASR 1001-X causes the QFP (QuantumFlow Processor) microcode to reload, briefly dropping all dataplane traffic. Pull crash details with `show platform software ess fp active` and `show platform hardware qfp active datapath utilization`. Look for QFP ucode tracebacks in `dir crashinfo:` for `qfp_ucode_*.crash`. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Identify the crash file: `dir crashinfo:` and `dir bootflash:tracelogs/`. Copy the `.crash` file off the device to TAC: `copy crashinfo:qfp_ucode_2026xxxx.crash tftp:`. Step 2: Run `show diag chassis eeprom detail` and `show platform` to confirm hardware status — look for a degraded ESP slot. Step 3: Check current IOS-XE version against known QFP-related defects on the Cisco Bug Search Tool — QFP crashes are commonly software defects in specific 16.x or 17.x trains. Step 4: If a fix is available in a newer release, schedule a maintenance window to upgrade: copy the new `.bin` to bootflash, set `boot system flash bootflash:asr1001x-universalk9.17.x.bin`, write memory, and reload. Step 5: Capture forwarding statistics for the last 24 hours: `show platform hardware qfp active datapath utilization summary` — sustained QFP utilisation above 75% is a known crash trigger. Step 6: As a workaround for a known software bug, disable the offending feature (often NetFlow or zone-based firewall) and reapply once upgraded. Step 7: Open a TAC case with the crash file, `show tech-support`, and the QFP utilisation history.

issue: HA Out-of-Sync and Override Disabled
search_summary: FortiGate FortiOS HA high availability out of sync checksum mismatch override A-A A-P heartbeat interface.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: An out-of-sync FortiGate HA cluster on the 100F is usually caused by a configuration checksum mismatch between primary and secondary, FortiGuard signature drift, or a flapping heartbeat interface. Run `diagnose sys ha checksum cluster` to compare. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From the CLI of the primary, run `get system ha status` — note `Override` setting and which unit is `Master`. Step 2: Compare configuration checksums: `diagnose sys ha checksum cluster` — any non-matching row identifies the mismatched section (global, root vdom, or specific config block). Step 3: Force a manual config sync from primary to secondary: `execute ha synchronize all`. Step 4: If the sync fails, force a full resync: `execute ha synchronize start` then `diagnose sys ha reset-uptime`. Step 5: Verify heartbeat health: `diagnose sys ha hadiag` and `get system ha status` — `hbdev` interfaces should show `up`. Replace heartbeat cables if link is flapping. Step 6: For FortiGuard signature drift (anti-virus / IPS DB versions differ), force a fresh update on both nodes: `execute update-now`. Step 7: If override is enabled and causing repeated failovers, disable it on the secondary: `config system ha` then `set override disable` and `end` — reboot the secondary so it does not preempt the primary. Step 8: Confirm sync restored: `diagnose sys ha checksum cluster` should show identical checksums on all rows.

issue: Dataplane CPU at 100 Percent and Session Throughput Drop
search_summary: Palo Alto PA-3220 dataplane CPU 100 percent session throughput drop packet loss high CPU PAN-OS show running resource.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A pegged dataplane CPU on the PA-3220 causes session establishment delays, dropped sessions, and throughput degradation. Pull current state with `show running resource-monitor minute` and `show running resource-monitor ingress-backlogs`. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From CLI, run `show running resource-monitor second last 60` to see CPU utilisation across all dataplane cores. Step 2: Identify hot cores: `show system resources` shows aggregate but `show running resource-monitor minute last 60` breaks it out per-core. Step 3: Check session usage: `show session info` — sessions near the platform max (1.2M for PA-3220) cause CPU spikes. Step 4: Find heavy sessions: `show session all filter count yes` and `show session info` — block any obvious DoS source IP with a deny rule at the top of the policy. Step 5: Check if SSL Decryption is the culprit: `show session info | match decrypt` — decrypt sessions are 5–8x more CPU-intensive. Consider excluding high-bandwidth flows from decryption with a No-Decrypt rule. Step 6: Check for abnormal application activity: `show running application` — large spikes in `app-id` for unknown-tcp or unknown-udp suggest a malicious flow. Step 7: If a recent PAN-OS upgrade is suspected, review release notes for known CPU regressions and consider a rollback. Step 8: For sustained issues, schedule packet captures filtered by the busiest source: `debug dataplane packet-diag set filter match source <ip>` and `set capture stage receive file rcv.pcap`.

issue: ASIC Hardware Drop and TCAM Exhaustion
search_summary: Arista 7280R3 forwarding ASIC hardware drop TCAM exhaustion route table overflow LPM J2 Jericho EOS.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: TCAM exhaustion on the Arista 7280R3 (Jericho2 ASIC) causes hardware drops and falls back to slow-path software forwarding. Confirm with `show platform jericho2 forwarding resource` and `show hardware capacity`. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Check current TCAM utilisation: `show hardware capacity utilization percent exceed 80` — anything in this list is a candidate. Common exhausted resources: `IPv4 LPM`, `IPv6 LPM`, `IPv4 ACL`, `MAC table`. Step 2: Identify route table size: `show ip route summary` — Jericho2 default profile supports ~400K IPv4 LPM entries. Step 3: Switch profiles if you need more LPM at the expense of ACL space: `hardware tcam profile l3xl` then `reload` (a reboot is required). Step 4: For ACL TCAM overflow, audit ACLs: `show ip access-lists summary` — collapse redundant rules. Use `tcam atomic` only when needed. Step 5: For MAC table overflow, check for L2 loops: `show interfaces mac-address-table` and `show spanning-tree blockedports`. Step 6: For BGP route bloat (full-table peers), apply BGP route filtering on inbound: `route-map FILTER-IN deny 10 / match ip address prefix-list LARGE-PREFIXES` to drop unneeded prefixes at ingress. Step 7: Confirm hardware drops have stopped: `show interfaces counters discards` — the `Hardware drops` counter should be stable.

issue: Both Units in Active State - Split Brain
search_summary: F5 BIG-IP active active split brain failover HA mirror sync TMSH cluster failover heartbeat unsync.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: Both BIG-IP units showing Active simultaneously (split brain) means the HA failover communication is broken and both units have taken over. This causes duplicate Virtual IPs and ARP conflicts on the network. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From each unit, run `tmsh show /sys failover` and `tmsh show /cm sync-status` to confirm both are claiming Active. Step 2: Identify the broken failover channel — F5 supports multiple: serial cable, dedicated network failover (preferred), and unicast peer addresses. Run `tmsh list cm device-group failover-mcast` and `tmsh list cm device <devicename> unicast-address`. Step 3: Verify failover network reachability between units: `ping <peer-failover-ip>` from each unit's failover address. Step 4: Force one unit to Standby immediately to stop the duplicate VIP issue: pick the unit that should be Standby and run `tmsh run /sys failover standby` — this will quiesce traffic on that unit. Step 5: Resync configuration from the desired master: `tmsh run /cm config-sync to-group <devicegroup>`. Step 6: Restore failover communication — fix any cable/switch port issue between failover NICs. Step 7: Re-test failover: `tmsh run /sys failover standby` on the active unit and confirm the standby promotes cleanly. Step 8: Verify mirror channel is healthy: `tmsh show /sys ha-status` — `Mirror` status must be `Connected` for connection mirroring to work.

issue: Datastore APD All Paths Down and VMs Inaccessible
search_summary: ESXi VMFS datastore APD all paths down PDL inaccessible VMs storage path failure HBA fibre channel iSCSI.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: An APD (All Paths Down) condition means the ESXi host has lost all paths to the storage array LUN but does not yet know if the LUN is permanently gone (PDL). VMs on the datastore become unresponsive but are not killed. Check with `esxcli storage nmp device list` and the vCenter Events tab. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Identify the affected datastore: in vSphere Client, look for the datastore with red status and 'Inaccessible' label. Step 2: SSH to the host and check path state: `esxcli storage core path list | grep -i state` — look for `dead` paths. Step 3: Check the storage adapter status: `esxcli storage core adapter list` and `esxcli storage san fc list` for HBA Link Status. Step 4: Verify physical SAN: confirm fibre cables seated, the FC switch zoning is correct, and the storage array LUN is presented to this host's WWN. Step 5: Force a path rescan: `esxcli storage core adapter rescan --all`. Step 6: If the LUN is permanently gone (decommissioned), declare PDL with `esxcli storage core device set --state=off --device=naa.xxxx` so the host stops retrying — but only after confirming no VMs are actively using it. Step 7: For VMs hung in inaccessible state, restart the management agents: `services.sh restart` (this does not affect running VMs but reconnects the host to vCenter). Step 8: After the storage path is restored, the datastore will auto-recover within a few minutes — confirm with `esxcli storage core device list -d naa.xxxx`.

issue: Service Fails to Start with Status Code 203
search_summary: Linux systemd service fails to start status 203 EXEC ExecStart not found permission denied SELinux unit file RHEL.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A systemd service failing with `status=203/EXEC` means systemd attempted to execute the ExecStart binary but the kernel returned ENOENT (file not found) or EACCES (permission denied). The unit loaded but the binary launch failed. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Get the failure reason: `systemctl status myservice.service -l` and `journalctl -u myservice.service --no-pager -n 50`. Step 2: Inspect the unit file: `systemctl cat myservice.service` — note the exact ExecStart= path. Step 3: Verify the binary exists at the exact path (no symlinks or relative paths) — systemd does not honour `$PATH`: `ls -lZ /full/path/to/binary`. Step 4: Confirm the binary is executable by the unit's `User=`: `sudo -u <serviceuser> /full/path/to/binary --version`. If permission denied, fix with `chmod 755 /full/path/to/binary` and `chown root:root` (or appropriate). Step 5: Check SELinux is not blocking it: `ausearch -m AVC -ts recent | grep myservice` — if AVCs are present, generate a policy module: `audit2allow -a -M myservice` and `semodule -i myservice.pp`. Step 6: For binaries in non-standard paths, ensure the SELinux context is `bin_t`: `semanage fcontext -a -t bin_t '/opt/myapp/bin(/.*)?'` then `restorecon -Rv /opt/myapp/bin`. Step 7: Reload systemd if the unit file changed: `systemctl daemon-reload` then `systemctl restart myservice.service`. Step 8: Confirm the service is active: `systemctl is-active myservice.service`.

issue: AD Replication Failure with Error 8606
search_summary: Active Directory replication failure error 8606 insufficient attributes lingering objects tombstone DC repadmin.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: AD replication error 8606 (`Insufficient attributes were given to create an object`) indicates lingering objects on a Domain Controller. The DC was offline longer than the tombstone lifetime (default 180 days) and is now trying to replicate stale objects. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Identify failing partners: open an elevated PowerShell on a healthy DC and run `repadmin /showrepl * /errorsonly`. Step 2: Confirm error 8606 in the output for the affected partition (e.g., `DC=corp,DC=local`). Step 3: Identify the source DC of the lingering objects: from a healthy DC, `repadmin /removelingeringobjects DC-Bad.corp.local <SourceDC-GUID> DC=corp,DC=local /advisory_mode` — this generates a report without making changes. Step 4: Review the report for the lingering object DNs in the directory service event log on the source DC. Step 5: After review, run the same command WITHOUT `/advisory_mode` from a healthy DC pointing at the bad DC to actually clean: `repadmin /removelingeringobjects DC-Bad.corp.local <SourceDC-GUID> DC=corp,DC=local`. Step 6: Force replication: `repadmin /syncall DC-Bad /AdeP`. Step 7: Re-run `repadmin /showrepl DC-Bad /errorsonly` — the 8606 errors should be gone. Step 8: PREVENTION: Set `Strict Replication Consistency` registry key to 1 on all DCs: `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\NTDS\Parameters\Strict Replication Consistency = 1` (DWORD) — this prevents the situation in future.

issue: Line Card Power-On Failure and Diag Fail State
search_summary: Cisco Nexus 9508 line card power on diag fail module not detected hardware failure show module N9K Cloud Scale.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A line card on the Nexus 9508 failing to come online with `Diag Fail` state typically means the module failed self-diagnostics during boot, the supervisor cannot communicate with the LC, or the LC has a hardware fault. Check with `show module` and `show environment power`. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From the active supervisor, run `show module` — note the slot number and `Status` (powered-up vs powered-dn vs diag-fail). Step 2: Check power budget: `show environment power` — confirm the chassis has enough budget for the LC class (some 36-port 100G LCs require an N9K-PAC-3000W-B). Step 3: Check the LC's GOLD diagnostic log: `show diagnostic result module <slot> detail` — look for failed tests like `BootupPortLoopback`, `EOBCPortLoopback`. Step 4: Reseat the line card: from the supervisor, gracefully shut it down: `poweroff module <slot>`. Step 5: Loosen the captive screws on the LC, slide it out about 4 inches, wait 10 seconds, then slide it firmly back in until the ejector cams click. Step 6: Re-power: `no poweroff module <slot>` and watch `show module` until `Status` shows `ok`. Step 7: If the diag failure persists after reseat, swap the LC into a different slot to isolate slot vs LC failure. Step 8: For persistent failure on a known-good slot, the LC needs RMA — collect `show tech-support module <slot>` and open a TAC case.

issue: Switch Stuck in ONIE Boot Loop
search_summary: Dell PowerSwitch S5248F OS10 ONIE boot loop install failed network boot grub recovery firmware corrupt.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A Dell PowerSwitch S5248F looping in ONIE (Open Network Install Environment) means the OS10 image is missing, corrupt, or grub cannot find a valid boot partition. Console shows ONIE prompts repeatedly attempting `discover`. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Connect to the console port (RJ45 to RS232, 115200 8N1) and observe the boot sequence to confirm the failure point. Step 2: At the ONIE prompt, stop auto-discovery: `onie-discovery-stop`. Step 3: Configure a management IP: `ifconfig eth0 192.168.10.10/24` and `ip route add default via 192.168.10.1`. Step 4: Verify connectivity to the image server: `ping <tftp-server>`. Step 5: Manually install OS10 by URL: `onie-nos-install http://server/onie-installer-x86_64-dellemc_s5248f_c3538-r0.bin`. Step 6: ONIE will download, verify the SHA256, partition the SSD, and install OS10 — typical install time is 5–8 minutes. The switch reboots automatically. Step 7: After reboot, log in as `admin / admin` and immediately change the default password. Step 8: If install fails with `signature verification`, the image is for the wrong platform — confirm the SKU matches `S5248F-ON` exactly. Step 9: For repeated install failures, run an SSD self-test from ONIE: `smartctl -a /dev/sda` — bad sectors indicate the internal SSD has failed and the chassis needs RMA.

issue: CIMC Web Login Fails and SOL Hangs
search_summary: Cisco UCS C240 M5 CIMC web login fails authentication SOL hang baseboard management restart firmware factory reset.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A Cisco UCS C-Series CIMC that fails web login or hangs on Serial-over-LAN means the BMC is in an unresponsive state. Common triggers: stale browser certificate cache, locked-out account from too many failed attempts, or BMC firmware hang. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Try a different browser in private/incognito mode to rule out cached SSL state. Step 2: From the host OS (if available), reset CIMC: `ipmitool mc reset cold` (requires `OpenIPMI` driver loaded). Step 3: For account lockout, wait 30 minutes for auto-unlock or use the boot-time password reset: power off, hold the F8 key during POST to enter the CIMC Configuration Utility — set new admin password and 'Restore Factory Default'. Step 4: Soft-reset CIMC from the host without power-cycling: at POST press F8, navigate to 'CIMC Reset' and confirm. Step 5: For a fully hung CIMC: power down the server, pull the AC for 30 seconds, then reapply — the BMC re-initialises from cold. Step 6: For SOL hangs, on a working CIMC, deactivate any stuck SOL session: `scope sol` then `disconnect`. Step 7: If CIMC firmware is corrupt, re-flash from the Host Upgrade Utility (HUU) ISO: boot the HUU from virtual media and select 'Update CIMC' — this runs in a self-contained Linux without needing a working CIMC web interface. Step 8: Confirm CIMC is healthy: from a workstation, `ssh admin@<cimc-ip>` and run `show version` — firmware version should match the HUU you installed.

issue: Router Reboots Spontaneously Every Few Hours
search_summary: Mikrotik CCR2004 RouterOS spontaneous reboot watchdog kernel panic supout.rif logging system health temperature.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A Mikrotik CCR2004 spontaneously rebooting every few hours typically indicates a kernel panic (RouterOS bug), thermal shutdown, or PSU instability. Pull the supout file for analysis. RESOLUTION: Step 1: SSH in immediately after a reboot and check uptime: `/system resource print`. Step 2: Pull the system log buffer (RouterOS keeps the most recent log in memory across reboots if `system logging action default disk` is set): `/log print where topics~"critical"`. Step 3: Check temperature: `/system health print` — CCR2004 max safe is 80°C. If above, clean fan filters and verify all four fans are spinning. Step 4: Check PSU input: `/system health print` — voltages outside ±5% of nominal indicate PSU or upstream PDU issue. Step 5: Generate a supout for MikroTik support: `/system sup-output name=supout.rif` and download it via FTP/SCP. Step 6: Check for known RouterOS bugs: review the changelog of your current version and the next stable release on mikrotik.com — kernel panic fixes are common between minor versions. Step 7: For confirmed software bug, schedule an upgrade: `/system package update set channel=stable`, `/system package update check-for-updates`, `/system package update install`. Step 8: Enable persistent crash logging to disk: `/system logging action set disk file-name=disk1 disk-lines-per-file=5000`, then `/system logging add topics=critical action=disk1`. Step 9: For repeated panics on latest stable, downgrade to a long-term release with `/system package downgrade`.

issue: PoE Port Not Delivering Power to Endpoint
search_summary: Aruba 6300M PoE power over ethernet port not delivering 802.3af 802.3at 802.3bt class detection power budget AOS-CX.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: An Aruba 6300M PoE port not delivering power to a connected device usually means classification failed, the port hit the per-port limit, the chassis exceeded its PoE budget, or the port is administratively disabled for PoE. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Check the port PoE state from AOS-CX: `show power-over-ethernet interface 1/1/24`. Look for `Power Status: Delivering` vs `Searching` or `Denied`. Step 2: Check the chassis PoE budget: `show power-over-ethernet` — if `Available Power` is near zero, the budget is exhausted. Step 3: Check the LLDP/CDP class advertised by the endpoint: `show lldp neighbors detail interface 1/1/24` — endpoints request class 0–8 and the switch must have headroom for that class. Step 4: Verify PoE is enabled on the port: `interface 1/1/24` then `power-over-ethernet` (no shut equivalent for PoE). Step 5: For a 6300M, only certain ports are PoE-capable depending on the SKU — confirm `show power-over-ethernet brief` lists the port as PoE-eligible. Step 6: For Class 8 (90W) endpoints, ensure the switch has the appropriate PoE++ PSU (1050W or 3000W on the 6300M chassis). Step 7: Try a known-good PoE endpoint on the same port to isolate switch vs endpoint. Step 8: As a last resort, recover the port: `interface 1/1/24` then `shutdown` followed by `no shutdown`. Step 9: For chronic budget issues across the chassis, consider upgrading the PSU or moving non-critical PoE devices off the chassis.

issue: Disk Shelf Path Fault and Multipath Degraded
search_summary: NetApp FAS8300 disk shelf SAS path fault multipath degraded ACP IOM module shelf ID amber LED ONTAP.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A NetApp FAS8300 reporting a degraded multipath state and shelf path fault means one of the two SAS paths to a disk shelf is down. The system is still operational on the remaining path but is single-point-of-failure. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From clustershell, identify the affected shelf: `storage shelf show -fields op-status` — look for shelf with `degraded`. Step 2: Check both IOM modules on the shelf: `storage shelf show -shelf <id> -instance` — both IOM A and IOM B should show `OK`. Step 3: Identify the failing path: `storage path show` — paths in `non-optimised` or `down` state need attention. Step 4: Physically inspect the affected shelf — the IOM with a solid amber LED on its faceplate is the failing module. Step 5: Trace the SAS cable from the failing IOM back to the controller HBA — confirm both ends are seated firmly with the locking tab clicked. Step 6: Replace the SAS cable first (cheapest variable): a fresh QSFP-to-mini-SAS cable, ends seated until they latch. Step 7: Recheck path state: `storage path show` — both paths should be `optimised` within 30 seconds. Step 8: If the path remains down after a fresh cable, the IOM module needs replacement: from clustershell, `storage shelf module replace -shelf <id> -module <a|b>` (NetApp's guided IOM hot-swap procedure). Step 9: Confirm ACP heartbeat is also healthy: `storage shelf acp show` — both `Connected: true` for redundancy.

issue: Stack Member Down and Ring Broken
search_summary: Cisco Catalyst 3850 stack cable stackwise broken ring member down stack-port full half bandwidth show switch stack-ports.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A broken Catalyst 3850 stack ring means one or both stack cables on a switch failed, leaving the stack running on a half-bandwidth bus topology. Members downstream of the break may show as `Provisioned` or `Unprovisioned`. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From the active stack member, run `show switch stack-ports` — both ports per switch should be `OK`. A `Down` port identifies the broken cable. Step 2: Check stack ring redundancy: `show switch stack-ring speed` and `show switch stack-bandwidth` — full bandwidth requires both rings up. Step 3: Identify the physical cable — stack cables connect StackWise port 1 of switch N to StackWise port 2 of switch N+1, looping back. Step 4: Try reseating both ends of the suspect cable — push until the captive screws thread, then tighten finger-tight. Step 5: Recheck `show switch stack-ports` — if still down, swap with a known-good cable to isolate cable vs port. Step 6: For hot-swap: stack cables are hot-swappable but the stack will briefly reconverge — schedule a maintenance window if possible. Step 7: If a cable swap doesn't restore the link, the stack port hardware on either switch may be faulty — power down and inspect for bent pins. Step 8: Confirm full-bandwidth restoration: `show switch stack-bandwidth` should show `480 Gbps` (full StackWise-480) or platform max. Step 9: Verify all members rejoined: `show switch` — all members should show `Ready` state with their priorities.

issue: AP Offline in Dashboard with Solid Orange LED
search_summary: Meraki MR46 access point offline dashboard solid orange LED cloud connection DHCP DNS firewall MX whitelist.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A Meraki MR46 AP showing solid orange LED with an `offline` state in the Meraki dashboard means the AP has power and an Ethernet link but cannot reach the Meraki cloud. Common causes: DHCP failure, DNS failure, firewall blocking outbound 443/7351, or local proxy interception. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Confirm power and link: the AP LED should at minimum be solid orange (not off). Step 2: Connect a laptop to the same VLAN/SSID the AP would use and verify DHCP works — AP needs an IP from the same DHCP scope. Step 3: From the laptop, test DNS resolution: `nslookup n12.meraki.com` — the AP relies on DNS to find the cloud. Step 4: Test outbound 443 to Meraki cloud: `curl -v https://n12.meraki.com` — must return TLS handshake. Step 5: Check upstream firewall — Meraki APs need outbound TCP 80, 443, 7734, 7752, and UDP 7351 to the Meraki cloud. Whitelist `*.meraki.com` and the Meraki ASN range. Step 6: Disable any SSL inspection / TLS interception for Meraki destinations — the AP uses certificate pinning and breaks if intercepted. Step 7: Power-cycle the AP via the upstream PoE switch port: `interface gi1/0/X` then `power inline never` for 5 seconds, then `power inline auto`. Step 8: Watch the AP LED — orange flashing → white flashing → solid white = success. If it stays solid orange after 3 minutes, the cloud is still unreachable. Step 9: If still offline, factory reset by holding the AP reset pin for 30 seconds, then re-claim in the Meraki dashboard with the serial number.

issue: Battery Replacement Required and Self-Test Failure
search_summary: APC Smart-UPS SRT 5000 battery replacement required F02 self test failure runtime degraded RBC battery module hot swap.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: An APC Smart-UPS SRT 5000 displaying `Replace Battery` or failing self-test (F02) means one or more battery cells in the RBC have degraded below safe runtime. The UPS will continue to provide line power but cannot bridge an outage. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From the front panel display or the PowerChute Network Shutdown UI, confirm: Status > Battery > 'Replace Battery' alarm, and note the current Runtime Estimate (typically drops below 5 minutes when batteries are degraded). Step 2: Identify the correct RBC (Replacement Battery Cartridge) part number for the SRT 5000 — typically RBC44 / APCRBC140 (verify on the unit's label). Step 3: For hot-swap: the SRT 5000 supports hot-swap so connected load is not interrupted. Open the front bezel. Step 4: Loosen the battery module captive screws (no tools required for the front modules) and slide the old battery cartridge out — it is heavy (≈30 kg), use two people. Step 5: Slide the new RBC in until it clicks home and tighten the captive screws. Step 6: Reset the battery date and runtime calibration: from the front panel, Configuration > Battery > Replace Battery Date > set to today; then run a Calibration Test (UPS will discharge to ~25% to recalibrate runtime estimate). Step 7: Run a self-test: front panel > Test & Diag > UPS Self-Test — expect a `Pass` result and a clear of the F02 alarm. Step 8: Dispose of the old RBC at an authorised lead-acid recycler — APC offers a take-back program. Step 9: Update the maintenance log — RBCs typically last 3–5 years.

issue: Controller in Secondary State After Failover
search_summary: Pure Storage FlashArray X70 controller secondary state failover purity OS upgrade non-disruptive primary takeover.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A Pure Storage FlashArray //X70 controller stuck in `secondary` state after a planned or unplanned failover means the originally-primary controller has come back online but has not reclaimed primary role. This is normally fine — both controllers are active for I/O — but worth verifying. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From Purity CLI (SSH to the array virtual IP as `pureuser`): `purearray list --controller` — confirm both controllers show `ready` state and one is `primary`. Step 2: Check controller versions match: `purearray list --controller` shows Purity version per controller. Mismatched versions indicate an in-progress upgrade — let it complete. Step 3: Check connectivity to the secondary: `purehw list --controller` — all FC/iSCSI/Eth ports should be `ok`. Step 4: For unbalanced load, manually trigger a failback so the previously-primary takes over again: `purearray reload --controller CT0` (this fails CT0 over to CT1, then back). Note this is non-disruptive but performs an actual failover. Step 5: Monitor with `puremonitor --interval 5 controller` during the failover — host I/O should not see any path drops thanks to ALUA / multipathing. Step 6: Check the host side multipath configuration: each host should see paths to both CT0 and CT1 and treat them as Active/Optimised vs Active/Non-Optimised. On Linux: `multipath -ll`. Step 7: If a controller refuses to take primary role, gather a logset for Pure Support: `puresupport --logset` and upload via the support portal. Step 8: For Purity upgrades, always verify both controllers are healthy before starting: Pure's NDU (Non-Disruptive Upgrade) requires both at the same baseline.

issue: etcd Member Unhealthy and Cluster API Slow
search_summary: Kubernetes etcd member unhealthy cluster API slow consensus quorum disk latency fsync Raft kubeadm control plane.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: An unhealthy etcd member causes the Kubernetes API to slow down or time out, especially for write operations. etcd is sensitive to disk I/O latency — fsync above 10ms median causes leader election storms. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From any control-plane node, get etcd member status: `kubectl -n kube-system exec etcd-cp1 -- etcdctl --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key endpoint status -w table`. Step 2: Check member health: `etcdctl ... endpoint health --cluster` — any member returning `unhealthy` is the culprit. Step 3: Check disk latency on each control-plane node: `ioping -c 10 /var/lib/etcd` — etcd needs <10ms p99 fsync. Step 4: Check for excessive WAL size: `du -sh /var/lib/etcd` — typical health is <2GB; >8GB suggests defrag is needed. Step 5: Defragment the database (one member at a time): `etcdctl ... --endpoints=https://10.0.0.11:2379 defrag` — repeat for each member. Step 6: For a leader-election storm, identify leader changes: `etcdctl ... endpoint status -w table` — `LEADER` column should be stable; rapid changes indicate disk or network issues. Step 7: If a member is unrecoverable, remove it and re-add: `etcdctl member remove <id>` then `kubeadm reset` on the bad node and rejoin with `kubeadm join --control-plane`. Step 8: Always keep etcd on dedicated SSDs with low-latency storage — never share with general-purpose workloads. Step 9: Monitor with Prometheus `etcd_disk_wal_fsync_duration_seconds_bucket` — alert on p99 > 10ms.

issue: WAN Interface Flapping and CRC Errors
search_summary: Cisco ISR 4451-X WAN interface flapping CRC errors input errors layer 1 SFP fiber copper duplex mismatch.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A WAN interface flapping with rising CRC counters on the ISR 4451-X indicates a Layer 1 problem — bad cable, dirty fiber, duplex mismatch, or upstream provider issue. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Check the interface counters: `show interface gigabitEthernet 0/0/0` — note `CRC`, `input errors`, `runts`, `giants`, and `interface resets`. Step 2: Clear and re-baseline: `clear counters gi0/0/0` then wait 5 minutes and re-check — rising CRC immediately confirms an active L1 issue. Step 3: For copper: confirm both ends are auto-negotiating: `show interface gi0/0/0 | include duplex|speed`. A duplex mismatch (one side full, other half) causes CRC under load. Set both ends to `speed auto` and `duplex auto`, or hard-code both to the same value. Step 4: Try a different copper cable, ideally Cat6a, and a different port on the upstream switch/router. Step 5: For fiber: clean both endfaces with a 1.25mm one-click cleaner — most fiber CRC is dirt. Step 6: Verify Tx/Rx optical power: `show interface gi0/0/0 transceiver detail` — Rx below -14 dBm or above +5 dBm indicates fiber/SFP issue. Step 7: For provider-side issues (you control only one end), open a ticket with the carrier providing CRC counts and a clear baseline (e.g., '47 CRC errors in 5 minutes after clear counters'). Step 8: As a temporary mitigation, dampen the interface to prevent BGP flap impact: `interface gi0/0/0` then `dampening`. Step 9: For sustained CRC, replace the SFP and the patch cord before suspecting the chassis port.

issue: Fan Failure Alarm and Continuous Audible Beep
search_summary: Supermicro SYS-1029U fan module failure audible beep alarm IPMI thermal shutdown PWM hot swap chassis cooling.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A continuous audible beep on a Supermicro 1029U with a fan failure alarm in the IPMI log means one of the chassis fans has dropped below the minimum RPM threshold. The system runs the remaining fans at higher RPM to compensate, but a sustained failure can cause thermal shutdown. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From the IPMI web UI -> Server Health -> Sensor Reading, identify which fan (FAN1–FAN8 typically) is reporting `0 RPM` or `Critical`. Step 2: Open the chassis lid — fans are hot-swappable on the 1029U. Step 3: Locate the failed fan by its position label on the chassis fan tray. The failed fan will be the one not spinning. Step 4: Squeeze the fan release tabs and lift the fan module straight up out of the tray. Step 5: Insert the replacement fan module, ensuring the connector seats fully — you should feel a slight click. Step 6: Within 5 seconds, the new fan ramps to baseline RPM and the IPMI alarm clears automatically. Step 7: Confirm in IPMI sensor view that the fan now reads its expected RPM (typically 5,000–8,000 RPM at idle). Step 8: Acknowledge the SEL log entry: IPMI -> Server Health -> Event Log -> Clear (after replacement is confirmed). Step 9: For repeated fan failures in the same slot, the fan tray PWM connector may be intermittent — schedule a chassis fan tray replacement during the next maintenance window. Step 10: To silence the beep before fan replacement is possible (e.g., 3 AM on-call): IPMI -> Configuration -> Buzzer -> Disable temporarily, but DO NOT leave it off.

issue: WAL Archiver Failing and Disk Filling Up
search_summary: PostgreSQL WAL archiver failing disk full pg_wal archive_command exit code shipping replication backup pg_basebackup.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A failing PostgreSQL WAL archiver causes pg_wal/ to grow until the disk fills, at which point the database stops accepting writes. The archiver retries indefinitely until the `archive_command` succeeds. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Check archiver status: `SELECT * FROM pg_stat_archiver;` — `failed_count` rising and `last_failed_time` recent confirms the failure. Step 2: Check pg_wal disk usage: `du -sh $PGDATA/pg_wal` — compare to the disk free. Step 3: Find the actual archive failure reason in the PG log: `tail -200 $PGDATA/log/postgresql-*.log | grep -i archive`. Step 4: Common failures: (a) destination disk full on the archive target, (b) network/SSH credentials broken to the remote archive server, (c) `archive_command` returns non-zero, (d) the destination filesystem is read-only. Step 5: Manually test the `archive_command` outside Postgres as the postgres user: `su - postgres` then run the exact `archive_command` substituting `%p` and `%f` with a real WAL file from pg_wal/. Step 6: Fix the underlying issue — clear destination disk space, restore SSH keys, fix the destination mount, etc. Step 7: Once `archive_command` succeeds manually, the archiver auto-resumes within seconds — confirm `last_archived_time` updates in `pg_stat_archiver`. Step 8: For an emergency disk-full where the database has stopped: free up space FIRST (rotate logs, drop unused tables, expand the volume). NEVER manually delete files from pg_wal/ — use `pg_archivecleanup` against your archive destination instead, then `SELECT pg_switch_wal();` to checkpoint. Step 9: PREVENT recurrence: alert on `pg_stat_archiver.failed_count` increasing AND on pg_wal/ size > 50% of disk.

issue: Access Point Stuck in Discovery and Not Joining WLC
search_summary: Cisco WLC 9800 access point AP discovery not joining CAPWAP DHCP option 43 DNS country code certificate fall back.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: An AP stuck in CAPWAP discovery and not joining the 9800 WLC means DNS / DHCP option 43 is misconfigured, the WLC certificate has issues, or the AP country code mismatches. RESOLUTION: Step 1: From the WLC CLI, monitor AP join attempts: `debug capwap events enable` and `debug capwap errors enable` — watch for incoming attempts. Step 2: Verify the AP can reach the WLC via DHCP option 43 (vendor-specific) or DNS A-record `CISCO-CAPWAP-CONTROLLER.<localdomain>`. Step 3: From a switch port the AP is on, confirm DHCP option 43 is delivered: on an IOS switch, `show ip dhcp pool <name>` and look for `option 43`. Format is hex-encoded WLC management IP: `f1 04 0a 00 00 01` for `10.0.0.1`. Step 4: Verify the AP is in `READY` state on its console (if console-accessible) or via `capwap ap log` on the AP CLI. Step 5: Check certificate validity: `show wireless certificate` — the WLC SSC or MIC must be valid; AP MIC certs older than 2024 may need a workaround for new APs. Step 6: Country code mismatch is a silent rejection: `show wireless country` on the WLC vs the AP-Manufactured country. Reset AP to factory: from the AP CLI `clear capwap private-config` and reboot. Step 7: For a 'never-joined' new AP, verify it shows in `show ap summary` even briefly — if not, CAPWAP is being blocked by a firewall (UDP 5246/5247). Step 8: If the AP is in a different VLAN from the WLC, ensure routing is in place AND that any stateful firewall allows UDP 5246/5247 and DTLS. Step 9: Last-resort: factory-reset the AP by holding the mode button at power-on for 30 seconds.

issue: Storage Pool Crashed After Power Loss
search_summary: Synology RS3621xs+ SHR storage pool crashed power loss DSM volume mount fsck btrfs ext4 degraded RAID.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A Synology storage pool entering 'Crashed' state after a power loss means a member disk dropped during a write or the metadata is in an inconsistent state. The pool may still be readable but DSM marks it crashed to force inspection. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Log into DSM as admin -> Storage Manager -> Storage Pool. Confirm pool state and identify any 'Failed' or 'Initialised' disks. Step 2: For an SHR pool, check disk health: Storage Manager -> HDD/SSD -> select disk -> Health Info. Look for any drive showing 'Failing' or 'Critical'. Step 3: SSH into the NAS as admin (must be enabled in Control Panel -> Terminal & SNMP) and become root: `sudo -i`. Step 4: Identify the underlying mdadm array: `cat /proc/mdstat` and `mdadm --detail /dev/md2` (the data array is usually md2 or higher). Step 5: If a disk shows 'removed' but is physically present and healthy, re-add it: `mdadm /dev/md2 --add /dev/sata5p3` (replace device path with the actual partition). Step 6: For a btrfs filesystem, check integrity: `btrfs check --readonly /dev/md2` — DO NOT run repair without a backup. Step 7: For an ext4 filesystem, force a check: `e2fsck -fy /dev/md2`. Step 8: Once the underlying array is clean, in DSM go to Storage Pool -> Action -> Online Assemble or Repair as prompted. Step 9: After recovery, run a `Data Scrubbing` task from Storage Manager — this catches silent bit-rot. Step 10: PREVENT recurrence: connect the NAS to a sized UPS and configure DSM Control Panel -> Hardware & Power -> UPS for graceful shutdown.

issue: vCenter Web Client Unreachable - VPXD Stopped
search_summary: vCenter Server 8 VPXD service stopped web client unreachable VAMI port 5480 vsphere-client-svcs vmware-vpxd database.
resolution: DIAGNOSTIC: A vCenter Server 8 with the vSphere Client unreachable typically has the `vmware-vpxd` service stopped or in a crash loop. Common triggers: vCenter database (vPostgres) full, certificate expired, or a recent patch left a service incompatibility. RESOLUTION: Step 1: Browse to VAMI on port 5480 (`https://vcenter.fqdn:5480`) — VAMI is independent of vpxd and gives service-level visibility. Login as `root`. Step 2: Navigate to Services and look for `vpxd` status — likely `Stopped`. Note any other Stopped services in the dependency chain. Step 3: SSH into vCenter as root (enable SSH from VAMI -> Access if needed). Run `service-control --status --all` for a full picture. Step 4: Check vPostgres disk space: `df -h /storage/db` and `df -h /storage/seat` — full disks are the #1 cause of vpxd failure. Free space if needed by trimming `/storage/log/vmware/vpxd/`. Step 5: Check vpxd logs: `tail -200 /var/log/vmware/vpxd/vpxd.log` and `vpxd-profiler.log`. Step 6: Common log signatures: `Database server returned error code: 53100` = disk full; `expired certificate` = cert needs renewal via `certificate-manager`; `DB schema mismatch` = a patch did not complete. Step 7: Try starting services in order: `service-control --start vmware-vpostgres`, then `service-control --start vmware-vpxd`. Step 8: For certificate expiry, run `/usr/lib/vmware-vmca/bin/certificate-manager` -> Option 4 (Regenerate Machine SSL with VMCA) and follow prompts. Step 9: For DB issues that won't clear, restore from a vCenter file-based backup taken before the issue (VAMI -> Backup -> Restore). Step 10: PREVENT: schedule weekly file-based backups of vCenter (VAMI -> Backup -> Schedule).
